event: 80 years old
non-smoker
history of hypertension
presented to our facility with acute dyspnea
developed fecal incontinence
left hemiplegia
dextroversion
dysarthria
vomiting
last seen by her family members at around 1.00 p.m. the day before
transported to the emergency department of our hospital
impaired consciousness
Japan coma scale score of Ⅱ-10
head computed tomography (CT) findings showed right thalamus and putamen bleeding
treated conservatively
Glasgow coma scale score was 12
vital signs were: systolic blood pressure, 91 mmHg; respiratory rate, 24/min; and oxygen saturation of arterial blood (SpO2), 86%
oral hygiene of the patient was poor
neurological findings were suggestive of an intracranial lesion
physical findings were diminished breath sounds on the left side and coarse crackles in the right lung; however, a decrease in breath sounds was also apparent in the front of the chest
respiratory condition deteriorated rapidly in the emergency room
SpO2 level could not be maintained at 90% even after administration of oxygen using an oxygen mask with a reservoir bag at 10 L/min
Endotracheal intubation was performed and mechanical ventilation was initiated
Chest CT findings showed bilateral infiltration
admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) for the management of severe pneumonia
Blood test results showed a decreased leukocyte count (1900/μL)
mildly elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level (3.6 mg/dL)
respiratory failure with a partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood (PaO2) of 64 mmHg with supplemental oxygen administration of 4 L/min
hepatorenal function was normal
Chest radiography at admission showed extensive infiltration shadows, mainly in the left whole lung fields and in the right lower lung field
on admission, head CT showed a hematoma extending from the right basal ganglia and putamen to the thalamus
Chest CT findings at admission revealed consolidations admixture with ground-glass opacities mainly in the middle lobe of the right lung and the upper and lower lobes of the left lung
diagnosed with Mendelson's syndrome just after mechanical ventilation initiation
Sputum culture predominantly showed the presence of Streptococcus agalactiae and Klebsiella oxytoca in a concentration of ≥ 107 colony-forming units/mL
poor oral hygiene, aspiration bacterial pneumonia was considered to be a potential complication
leukocytopenia (white blood count [WBC], 1900/μL) and the low serum CRP level (3.6 mg/dL) on admission
Management in the ventilator mode was started using meropenem and levofloxacin
prednisolone was started at 1 mg/kg/day
infiltration shadows observed at admission improved by Day 12
P/F ratio improved and was maintained at ≥250, she was extubated on the same day
transferred to the Department of Neurosurgery on Day 22
transferred to a rehabilitation hospital for sequelae of stroke
